items.passage: Green plants convert sunlight into chemical energy through a remarkable process called photosynthesis. Inside the leaves, tiny structures known as chloroplasts capture light using a green pigment named chlorophyll. This trapped energy drives a reaction that combines water and carbon dioxide into simple sugars. Oxygen is released as a byproduct, which countless animals and humans depend on to breathe. Because it feeds the plant and enriches the air, photosynthesis remains essential to nearly all life on Earth.

Cities often feel noticeably warmer than the surrounding countryside, a phenomenon called the urban heat island. Dark roofs and paved roads absorb sunlight during the day and slowly release that stored heat at night. Tall buildings block cooling winds while air conditioners and traffic add even more warmth to the streets. Because concrete replaces vegetation, there are fewer trees to provide shade or release moisture through their leaves. Planners now add parks and reflective surfaces to help lower these rising urban temperatures.

Human color perception begins when light of different wavelengths enters the eye and strikes the retina. Special cells called cones respond to red, green, and blue light, sending signals to the brain. The brain then blends these signals so that people experience a rich range of distinct colors. Interestingly, the same object can appear to shift in color depending on the surrounding light and nearby shades. This shows that color is not simply a property of objects but a construction of the mind.

The Industrial Revolution transformed how goods were produced when machines began to replace handmade work. Powered by steam and coal, new factories concentrated thousands of workers under a single roof for the first time. Railways and canals soon carried raw materials and finished products across entire nations at unheard-of speeds. As cities swelled with families seeking wages, crowded housing and long hours became common features of daily life. These sweeping changes eventually reshaped economies, societies, and the ordinary rhythms of work everywhere.

Fossils are the preserved remains or traces of organisms that lived many thousands of years ago. Most form when a dead plant or animal is quickly buried by mud, sand, or volcanic ash. Over long periods, minerals seep into the buried tissue and slowly harden it into solid rock. By studying these stone records, scientists can reconstruct ancient environments and track how species changed over time. Each fossil therefore offers a rare window into the distant history of life on this planet.

Dwarf planets are round worlds that orbit the Sun but have not cleared their neighborhood of debris. Pluto is the most famous example, though astronomers have since identified several others beyond the orbit of Neptune. Like true planets, they are massive enough for their own gravity to pull them into a spherical shape. However, they share their orbital paths with many icy bodies rather than dominating the space around them. Their discovery forced scientists to rethink exactly what the word planet should mean.
